name: Iliad
shortDescription: Homer's epic poem recounting the Trojan War and Achilles' wrath.
description: The Iliad, attributed to Homer, is an ancient Greek epic poem set during the Trojan War, a ten-year siege of the city of Troy (Ilium) by a coalition of Greek kingdoms. It focuses on a few weeks in the final year of the war and centers on the rage of Achilles, the greatest of the Greek warriors. The poem explores themes of war, honor, fate, and the intervention of the gods in human affairs. Key episodes include the quarrel between Achilles and Agamemnon, Hector's defense of Troy, and the death of Patroclus, which spurs Achilles to return to battle and slay Hector. The Iliad provides profound insights into the values and beliefs of ancient Greek society, offering a vivid portrayal of heroic ideals and the tragic consequences of human conflict.
subtitle: Of wrath, gods, and the siege of Troy.
author: Homer (attributed)
significance: Foundation of Western Literature; insight into ancient Greek culture.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The Iliad is traditionally attributed to Homer, a possibly legendary bard who is also credited with composing the Odyssey. Its origins lie in a rich oral tradition of epic poetry that predates the written text. The mythological backdrop of the Iliad is the Trojan War, sparked by the abduction of Helen, wife of Menelaus, king of Sparta, by Paris, a prince of Troy. This act ignited the wrath of the Achaeans (Greeks), who sailed to Troy to reclaim Helen and avenge the insult. The gods and goddesses of Olympus are heavily involved in the conflict, taking sides and influencing the course of events. Zeus, Hera, Athena, Apollo, and Poseidon are among the deities who play significant roles in the poem.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: The Iliad held immense cultural and religious significance for the ancient Greeks. It served as a primary source of moral and ethical instruction, shaping their understanding of heroism, duty, and the relationship between humans and the divine. The poem was recited at festivals and gatherings, reinforcing communal values and a shared sense of identity. The Iliad also functioned as a historical record, albeit one infused with myth and legend, preserving the memory of a glorious past and the deeds of legendary heroes. Moreover, it provided a framework for understanding the world and the forces that governed human existence, including fate, divine intervention, and the consequences of hubris.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: The Iliad is rich in symbolism and open to various interpretations. The Trojan War itself can be seen as a symbol of human conflict and the destructive nature of war. Achilles' rage represents the destructive power of unchecked emotions and the consequences of pride. Hector's defense of Troy symbolizes the importance of duty, honor, and familial loyalty. The interventions of the gods represent the capricious and often arbitrary nature of fate. The poem can also be interpreted as a meditation on mortality, the fleeting nature of glory, and the enduring power of human relationships. The shield of Achilles, described in detail in Book 18, is a microcosm of the world, representing the interconnectedness of human life and the forces of nature.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The Iliad has exerted a profound and enduring influence on Western literature, art, and culture. It has served as a model for epic poetry and a source of inspiration for countless writers, artists, and composers. The characters and stories of the Iliad have been retold and reinterpreted in various forms, from classical tragedies to modern novels and films. The themes of war, honor, love, and loss continue to resonate with audiences today. The Iliad has also shaped our understanding of ancient Greece and its contribution to Western civilization. Its influence can be seen in fields such as philosophy, history, and political science. The poem remains a testament to the power of storytelling and the enduring relevance of ancient myths.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: The primary source for the Trojan War and the rage of Achilles.

sources.author: Thucydides
sources.description: A historical account that references the Trojan War and its significance.

associations.name: Bronze
associations.significance: The metal of weapons and armor in the Iliad's era.

associations.name: Kléos
associations.significance: Glory, fame, and reputation earned through heroic deeds.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Mahabharata
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Epic poem about a great war with divine interventions and heroic figures.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Shahnameh
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Epic poem that recounts the mythical and historical past of Persia, featuring heroic warriors and conflicts.

companions.name: Achilles
companions.description: The central hero of the Iliad, known for his unparalleled martial prowess and his wrath.

companions.name: Hector
companions.description: The Trojan prince and greatest warrior, representing the defense of Troy and familial duty.

companions.name: Agamemnon
companions.description: King of Mycenae and leader of the Achaean army.

worshipPractices.name: Libations
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of liquids to the gods, seeking their favor or appeasement.

worshipPractices.name: Sacrifices
worshipPractices.description: Animal sacrifices performed to honor the gods and seek their blessings.

festivals.name: Panathenaia
festivals.description: A major Athenian festival that included recitations of the Iliad and other epic poems.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Iliad continues to inspire literature, art, film, and scholarship, shaping our understanding of heroism, war, and the human condition.
